one_line_takeaway: A five-month, 786K-app measurement showing LLM app stores host pervasive and unevenly-policed abuse -- from mismatched descriptions to 616 apps that generate real malware/phishing with plain prompts -- and that store moderation is empirically evadable.
key_finding: Insecurity is widespread but highly uneven across stores. Of 42,892 apps with both description and instructions, 35.31% showed description-instruction inconsistency and 57.38% of those contained harmful content. The intersection detector flagged 15,996 apps (27.91% of examined apps) as having malicious instructions, dominated by sexual, violence, and profanity content. 616 apps (1.96%) were dynamically confirmed to execute real malicious behavior using ordinary prompts. Per-store malicious-intent rates diverge sharply -- FlowGPT 54.28%, Character.AI 25.78%, Poe 20.32%, GPT Store only 3.19% -- and store moderation is shown to be evadable via the authors' own disguised-app deployments.
core_contribution: The first large-scale empirical security measurement of LLM app stores. The authors propose a three-layer 'concern framework' (apps with abusive potential, apps with malicious intent, and apps with exploitable vulnerabilities) and build an automated static+dynamic pipeline to instantiate it across 786,036 apps crawled over five months from six stores (GPT Store, FlowGPT, Poe, Coze, Cici, Character.AI). A concrete engineering artifact is ToxicDict, a custom toxic-word dictionary of 31,783 entries spanning 14 categories and 8 languages, built from store policies, public datasets, GPT-4o expansion, and GPT-4o translation. They contrast their work with prior study of explicitly-malicious PAID LLM services (Lin et al.), arguing that the low-cost, high-volume app-store setting is a distinct and larger attack surface.
threat_model: Malicious or negligent app developers exploit weak app-store vetting. Layer 1 (abusive potential): developers publish apps whose public description does not match their hidden instructions (the app's 'source code'), over-collect sensitive user data via third-party 'actions' beyond what privacy policies declare, or operate from disreputable author domains; victims are individual users misled or having data mishandled. Layer 2 (malicious intent): developers deliberately embed harmful functionality (hate, sexual, violent, extremist content, etc.) in instructions or knowledge files. Layer 3 (exploitable vulnerability): benign-looking apps can be driven, using ordinary (non-jailbreak) prompts, to produce malware, phishing, data exfiltration, DoS, or disinformation; and attackers can upload malicious knowledge files (e.g. phishing-URL corpora) and use workspace/link visibility to selectively distribute harmful content while evading moderation. Attacker capability is that of a normal store user/developer; no privileged access assumed.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) the six stores may not represent the broader ecosystem, so the picture is incomplete; (2) findings depend on store-provided metadata quality -- stores that hide instructions/descriptions (Coze yielded instructions for only 1,491 of 51,918 apps; Cici lacks instructions entirely) could not be fully assessed, so their 0% rates are artifacts of missing data, not safety; (3) predefined criteria and automated tools may miss nuanced malice, partially mitigated by manual verification and self-refinement. Reviewer-noted: the knowledge-file result rests on only 559 downloadable CSVs (platform restrictions), toxicity-score summation across 14 categories is somewhat ad hoc, Character.AI/Cici were keyword-seeded from ToxicDict which inflates their apparent malicious ratio (authors acknowledge this), the malicious-intent 'ground truth' is itself LLM-judged (Llama3-8B) rather than human-validated at scale, and data/tools were only promised public 'upon acceptance.'

datasets_used: 786,036 LLM apps crawled over five months from six stores (GPT Store 663,119; Coze 51,918; FlowGPT 34,345; Poe 16,544; Cici 13,060; Character.AI 7,050)
GPTZoo dataset (metadata for 730,420 GPT Store apps) used to seed GPT Store collection
57,317 apps for which instructions were retrieved (the malicious-intent detection base)
42,892 apps with both description and instructions (24,796 FlowGPT, 12,234 GPT Store, 5,862 Poe) for inconsistency analysis
ToxicDict: custom-built dictionary of 31,783 toxic words, 14 categories, 8 languages
192,714 knowledge files across 45,690 GPT Store apps (only 559 CSVs downloadable)
5,498 apps with third-party action usage; 32 sensitive data types reference list
quantitative_results: Finding 1: 35.31% of 42,892 apps had description-instruction inconsistency; 57.38% of inconsistent apps contained harmful content
Finding 2: 1,688 actions (29.27%) over-collected sensitive data, mostly among obscure third-party services

Finding 3: of 4,264 apps from 677 malicious/suspicious author domains (507 malicious + 215 suspicious, 9.47% of 7,623 valid domains), only 106 (2.49%) had malicious intent -> domain reputation is an unreliable predictor
Finding 4: malicious-intent detection intersection = 15,996 apps (27.91% of examined), union = 31,494; dominant categories sexual/violence/profanity
Finding 5: 198 of 559 examined knowledge files (35.42%) contained malicious content
Finding 6: 616 apps (1.96%) confirmed to execute malicious behavior -- malware generation 198 (0.63%), DoS 172 (0.55%), disinformation 171 (0.54%), data exfiltration 47 (0.15%), phishing 28 (0.09%)
Finding 7: 287 malicious-intent apps across 227 workspaces, 24 workspaces holding 2+ malicious apps
Per-store malicious intent (Table IX): FlowGPT 54.28% (13,562/24,983), Character.AI 25.78%, Poe 20.32%, GPT Store 3.19%; abusive potential highest at Poe 52.85%
Contribution totals: 16,376 abusive-potential, 15,996 malicious-intent, 616 exploitable-vulnerability apps
During crawl 5,462 apps were removed, 132 likely for policy violations; Character.AI had 54.58% of 469 malicious apps with >5,000 interactions, peak 31,763,232 interactions
baselines_compared: No competing detection system is benchmarked against (this is a measurement study, not a classifier bake-off). Positioned against prior work: Lin et al.'s study of explicitly-malicious PAID LLM services (costly, limited number) vs. this paper's low-cost, high-volume free app-store setting; Zhao et al.'s LLM-app-store vision/roadmap; Hou et al.'s GPTZoo dataset (reused as input).

Internal cross-check: self-refining Llama3-8B toxic detector vs. rule-based ToxicDict matching, reported as 'largely consistent' with intersection (15,996) chosen as the final result over union (31,494).

Detection components leveraged as oracles: VirusTotal, Google Safe Browsing, Polisis; effectiveness graded by CRR/FC/CC/CA/MEE using PyLint, ESLint, Cppcheck, Cuckoo Sandbox, Wireshark, VirusTotal, and human raters.
